full_name: Artificial Intelligence and Data Act
issuing_body: Parliament of Canada / Government of Canada (Innovation, Science and Industry Canada)
scope_summary: The Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) is the proposed AI law in Bill C-27 that would regulate international and interprovincial trade and commerce in artificial intelligence systems. It would apply primarily to persons responsible for high-impact AI systems, requiring risk assessment, mitigation, monitoring, record-keeping, transparency, and reporting duties, and it also creates offence provisions for certain harmful or unlawful AI-related conduct.
applies_to: Persons carrying out regulated activities in international or interprovincial trade and commerce: organizations that process data for AI design/development/use, or design, develop, make available for use, or manage the operation of AI systems. Core obligations focus on high-impact AI systems; government institutions and certain national-security controlled activities are excluded.

full_name: Australia’s AI Ethics Principles and Voluntary AI Safety Standard
issuing_body: Department of Industry, Science and Resources (National AI Centre / Australian Government)
effective_date: 2019-11-07 (AI Ethics Principles); 2024-09-05 / 2025-10-21 page update for Voluntary AI Safety Standard — voluntary
scope_summary: Australia’s AI Ethics Principles provide a voluntary ethical baseline for designing, developing, and implementing AI in ways that are safe, fair, transparent, accountable, and human-centred. The Voluntary AI Safety Standard is a voluntary implementation guide for industry that sets out practical guardrails for organisations using, developing, or supplying AI systems, especially where higher-risk applications are involved.
applies_to: Australian industry and government use cases referenced by the principles; the voluntary standard is aimed at organisations using, developing, supplying, or procuring AI systems, including businesses of all sizes and sectors, especially higher-risk deployments.

full_name: Projeto de Lei nº 2.338, de 2023 (Marco Legal da Inteligência Artificial) and Lei nº 13.709/2018 (Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados Pessoais - LGPD)
issuing_body: Brazilian National Congress (PL 2338/2023); Presidency of the Republic / National Data Protection Authority (LGPD)
scope_summary: PL 2338/2023 is Brazil’s federal AI bill and, as reflected on the Senate’s official page, it was approved by the Senate in substitute form and sent to the Câmara dos Deputados for revision, so it is not yet in force as law. The bill covers general AI governance, high-risk systems, impact assessments, human oversight, auditability, and liability themes for AI systems used in Brazil. The LGPD is Brazil’s in-force federal personal data law and applies broadly to processing of personal data in Brazil or targeting individuals in Brazil, including AI systems that process personal data, make automated decisions, or rely on sensitive data.
applies_to: PL 2338/2023 applies to AI systems and related providers/deployers in Brazil, especially systems categorized as high risk, generative AI where covered, and uses affecting rights, safety, or public interests. LGPD applies to any natural person, public entity, or private legal entity processing personal data in Brazil or targeting individuals in Brazil, across all sectors and organization sizes.

full_name: Interim Measures for the Management of Generative AI Services
issuing_body: Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC), jointly with the National Development and Reform Commission, Ministry of Education, Ministry of Science and Technology, Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, Ministry of Public Security, and National Radio and Television Administration
scope_summary: Applies to generative AI services that use generative AI technology to provide text, image, audio, or video content to the public in mainland China. It covers service providers and their obligations around lawful training data, content safety, transparency, user protections, labeling, complaint handling, and regulatory filing; purely internal R&D not offered to the public is outside scope.
applies_to: Organizations, platforms, and individuals providing generative AI services to the public in mainland China, including services delivered through APIs; applies regardless of industry, especially for services with public-opinion influence or social-mobilization capacity. Pure internal development, research, or testing not offered to the public is generally out of scope, but downstream providers may still have obligations when they launch public services.

full_name: Consumer Protections for Artificial Intelligence
issuing_body: Colorado General Assembly; enforced by the Colorado Attorney General
effective_date: May 17, 2024 (with key AI duties effective February 1, 2026)
scope_summary: Colorado SB 24-205 regulates developers and deployers of high-risk artificial intelligence systems that make or substantially factor into consequential decisions affecting Colorado consumers. It requires risk management, impact assessments, disclosures, consumer notice and appeal rights, and reporting to the Attorney General, with separate consumer-facing disclosure duties for AI systems that interact with consumers.
applies_to: Persons doing business in Colorado that develop or deploy high-risk artificial intelligence systems; also any person making available an AI system intended to interact with consumers. High-risk systems are those that make or substantially factor into consequential decisions affecting education, employment, financial services, housing, insurance, health care, legal services, or essential government services. The statute generally targets business entities rather than a specific size threshold, though deployer reasonableness considers size and complexity.

full_name: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) No 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act) (Text with EEA relevance)
issuing_body: European Parliament and Council of the European Union; enforced by the European Commission and national competent authorities
effective_date: 2024-08-01 (entered into force); applies in phases through 2027 and beyond
scope_summary: The EU AI Act is the European Union’s horizontal AI law, setting harmonised rules for the placing on the market, putting into service, and use of AI systems. It bans certain unacceptable-risk practices, imposes detailed obligations on high-risk AI systems and general-purpose AI models, and adds transparency, governance, market surveillance, and enforcement requirements for providers, deployers, importers, distributors, and other operators.
applies_to: Providers, deployers, importers, distributors, product manufacturers, authorized representatives, and affected operators of AI systems in the EU; especially high-risk AI systems and general-purpose AI model providers. Scope also reaches certain AI practices regardless of sector, plus public authorities and some third-country operators where outputs are used in the EU.

full_name: Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data, and repealing Directive 95/46/EC
issuing_body: European Parliament and Council of the European Union; enforced by national data protection authorities and the European Data Protection Board
scope_summary: The GDPR is the EU’s general data protection law. Articles 22 and 35 are especially relevant to AI governance because they govern solely automated decision-making with legal or similarly significant effects and require a data protection impact assessment for processing that is likely to create high risk to individuals, including certain profiling-based systems. The rules apply to controllers and processors handling personal data in the EU context, including many AI systems that use personal data for scoring, ranking, eligibility, or similar decisions.
applies_to: Controllers and processors in the EU/EEA processing personal data; especially AI/ML systems used for profiling, scoring, eligibility, fraud, hiring, credit, insurance, marketing, surveillance, or any decision-making with legal or similarly significant effects. Applies across sectors and organization sizes, with additional sensitivity where special-category data, large-scale processing, or publicly accessible area monitoring is involved.

full_name: HIPAA Security Rule
issuing_body: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office for Civil Rights (OCR)
scope_summary: The HIPAA Security Rule establishes national standards to protect individuals’ electronic protected health information (ePHI) that is created, received, used, or maintained by covered entities and their business associates. It requires administrative, physical, and technical safeguards to ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI, and it applies across the health care sector and group health plans to the extent they handle ePHI.
applies_to: Covered entities, business associates, and group health plans in the U.S. health care ecosystem that create, receive, maintain, or transmit ePHI; all sizes and all AI/ML systems insofar as they access, process, store, or transmit ePHI.

full_name: IEEE/ISO/IEC International Standard—Systems and software engineering—Life cycle management—Part 7000: Standard model process for addressing ethical concerns during system design
issuing_body: IEEE Standards Association
scope_summary: IEEE 7000-2021 establishes a model process for addressing ethical concerns during system design by integrating ethical values into concept exploration, development, and requirements definition. It is intended for organizations of all sizes and types that use their own life cycle models, including those working on autonomous intelligent systems or other systems where human and social values need to be translated into design decisions.
applies_to: All sizes and types of organizations; systems and software engineering teams; organizations developing or revising products or services; especially relevant to autonomous intelligent systems and other AI-enabled or high-impact socio-technical systems.

full_name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
issuing_body: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (Government of India); enforced by the Data Protection Board of India
effective_date: 2023-08-11 (enactment); operative provisions implemented via notified rules / notifications
scope_summary: India's national personal data protection law for digital personal data. It applies to processing of digital personal data in India and to certain processing outside India when connected to offering goods or services to individuals in India. For AI systems, it functions as a governance baseline wherever an AI system processes personal data, requiring lawful basis, notice, consent management, security safeguards, breach reporting, and stronger controls for children and significant data fiduciaries.
applies_to: Any organization processing digital personal data in India; also extraterritorial processing tied to offering goods or services to individuals in India. In practice this covers most consumer, employee, platform, SaaS, fintech, health, telecom, and AI/ML systems that ingest or infer from personal data, regardless of company size; enhanced duties can apply to entities notified as Significant Data Fiduciaries.

full_name: ISO/IEC 23894:2023 Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Guidance on risk management
issuing_body: ISO/IEC (published by ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 42)
scope_summary: ISO/IEC 23894:2023 provides guidance for organizations that develop, produce, deploy, or use products, systems, and services that utilize artificial intelligence (AI) to manage AI-related risk. It is intended to help organizations integrate AI risk management into their AI-related activities and functions, and the application can be customized to any organization and context.
applies_to: Organizations of any size in any sector that develop, produce, deploy, or use AI-enabled products, systems, or services.

full_name: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Management system
issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC), via ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 42
scope_summary: ISO/IEC 42001 is an international management system standard for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an Artificial Intelligence Management System (AIMS). It is intended for organizations of any size and across all industries that develop, provide, or use AI-based products or services, including public sector bodies, companies, and non-profits.
applies_to: Organizations of any size across all industries that develop, provide, or use AI-based products or services; applicable to public sector agencies, companies, and non-profits.

full_name: Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust Foundation
issuing_body: Ministry of Science and ICT (Republic of Korea)
scope_summary: This act establishes South Korea’s national AI governance framework, including a presidential AI committee, policy support bodies, learning-data policies, trust and safety measures, and rules for high-impact and generative AI. It applies broadly to AI activities affecting the domestic market or users, including some extraterritorial conduct, while excluding AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security as prescribed by Presidential Decree.
applies_to: AI developers, providers, and business operators whose activities affect the Korean market or Korean users, including foreign operators above applicable thresholds; the Act also creates special obligations for high-impact AI, generative AI, and AI systems meeting the prescribed computation threshold. State and local governments, designated committees, policy centers, and safety institutions are also addressed for governance and support functions.

full_name: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
issuing_body: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
scope_summary: A voluntary, cross-sector framework for organizations that design, develop, deploy, or use AI systems to manage AI-related risks and promote trustworthy and responsible AI. It addresses trustworthiness considerations across the AI lifecycle, including governance, mapping context and impacts, measurement of risks and performance, and ongoing management. It is intended to be adaptable across industries, organization sizes, and AI use cases.
applies_to: Any organization that designs, develops, deploys, evaluates, or uses AI systems; applicable across sectors, organization sizes, and AI model types, including generative AI. It is guidance rather than a legal requirement and is meant to be adapted to specific use cases.

full_name: New York City Local Law 144 of 2021, amending the Administrative Code of the City of New York in relation to automated employment decision tools
issuing_body: New York City Council; enforced by the NYC Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP)
scope_summary: Local Law 144 regulates employers and employment agencies that use automated employment decision tools (AEDTs) to screen candidates or employees for hiring or promotion decisions in New York City. It requires an annual independent bias audit, public disclosure of audit results, and advance notice to affected New York City residents before an AEDT is used.
applies_to: Employers and employment agencies that use AEDTs in New York City for hiring or promotion screening, including jobs located in NYC at least part time, fully remote roles tied to a NYC office, and employment agencies in NYC or otherwise connected to such NYC roles. It applies to companies of any size when the AEDT is used to substantially assist or replace discretionary decision-making in employment decisions affecting natural persons.

full_name: Recommendation of the Council on Artificial Intelligence
issuing_body: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Council
jurisdiction: Global / international (OECD Adherents and non-Adherents invited to take account)
scope_summary: This OECD Recommendation sets out principles for trustworthy AI and policy guidance for governments and AI actors. It applies across sectors and AI system lifecycles, focusing on responsible stewardship, human rights, transparency, robustness, accountability, and international interoperability rather than sector-specific mandates.
applies_to: OECD member countries, adhering partner economies, and non-member jurisdictions invited to take account of the Recommendation; also used as a reference by AI developers, deployers, suppliers, users, and policymakers across sectors and organization sizes.

full_name: Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act
issuing_body: California Legislature; enforced by the California Attorney General and Office of Emergency Services
effective_date: TBD — enacted Sep. 29, 2025; operative date not stated in the cited official page
scope_summary: California’s post-veto successor framework to SB 1047 is SB 53 / the Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act. It regulates frontier AI developers and, for the strongest obligations, large frontier developers, requiring public transparency frameworks, incident reporting, whistleblower protections, and periodic updates to the law. The framework focuses on models trained above a compute threshold and developers above a revenue threshold, while also creating CalCompute to expand safe and equitable access to compute.
applies_to: Frontier AI developers training frontier models above 10^26 integer or floating-point operations; large frontier developers with annual gross revenue above $500 million; applies statewide in California and includes both public transparency obligations and confidential reporting obligations. Core obligations are aimed at frontier model developers, while whistleblower protections apply to covered employees involved in assessing or addressing critical safety risk.

full_name: Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI
issuing_body: Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) and AI Verify Foundation
scope_summary: A voluntary, sector-agnostic governance framework for organisations developing, deploying, or using generative AI systems. It sets out nine dimensions to be considered in totality so stakeholders can manage GenAI risks while supporting innovation, with emphasis on explainability, human oversight, accountability, testing, security, provenance, and data protection.
applies_to: Private-sector organisations and other stakeholders developing, deploying, or using generative AI in Singapore; sector- and technology-agnostic; relevant across organization sizes where GenAI is used, including model developers, deployers, policymakers, research community, and end users.

full_name: 2017 Trust Services Criteria for Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy (With Revised Points of Focus – 2022)
issuing_body: AICPA’s Assurance Services Executive Committee (ASEC)
jurisdiction: United States; used internationally in SOC 2 engagements
scope_summary: The Trust Services Criteria are AICPA control criteria used in attestation or consulting engagements to evaluate and report on controls over the security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, or privacy of information and systems used to provide products or services. They are designed for service organizations and can be applied across an entire entity or to a subsidiary, division, or operating unit, including systems used to process users’ data.[AICPA](https://www.aicpa-cima.com/resources/download/2017-trust-services-criteria-with-revised-points-of-focus-2022)
applies_to: Service organizations of any size and industry that provide products or services using systems subject to SOC 2 reporting; commonly cloud, SaaS, data processing, managed services, fintech, healthcare vendors, and other outsourced service providers. Can cover one or more trust services categories (security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, privacy) and may be scoped to an entity, subsidiary, division, or operating unit.

full_name: Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act
issuing_body: Texas Legislature; enforced by the Texas Attorney General and related state agencies
scope_summary: Proposed Texas state legislation governing the development, deployment, disclosure, testing, and enforcement of artificial intelligence systems used by covered businesses, deployers, developers, and certain state agencies in Texas. It establishes consumer disclosure duties, prohibited AI uses, a regulatory sandbox, a Texas Artificial Intelligence Council, and Attorney General enforcement with civil penalties and injunctive relief.
applies_to: Persons or business entities that promote, advertise, or conduct business in Texas; produce products or services used by Texas residents; or develop or deploy AI systems in Texas. The bill also applies to certain Texas government agencies, and it creates a sandbox for innovative AI testing with limited exemptions. It is broad across sectors, including healthcare, finance, education, and public services, with specific heightened restrictions for government uses and consumer-facing interactions.

full_name: A pro-innovation approach to AI regulation
issuing_body: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT), UK Government
scope_summary: This is the UK government’s proposed cross-sector AI governance framework, intended to guide how existing UK regulators interpret and apply five common AI principles within their own remits. It is context-based and non-statutory in its current form, covering AI use across sectors while leaving defence, national security, and other existing exemptions to sector-specific regimes.
applies_to: All UK sectors using AI, with implementation by existing sector regulators; especially relevant to developers and deployers of high-impact AI, highly capable general-purpose AI systems, and public-sector bodies subject to regulator guidance or procurement/assurance standards. Exemptions or tailored treatment may apply where existing regimes already address unique operating requirements, including defence and national security.

full_name: Seizing the opportunities of safe, secure and trustworthy artificial intelligence systems for sustainable development
issuing_body: United Nations General Assembly
scope_summary: This General Assembly resolution sets out a global policy framework for the safe, secure, and trustworthy use of AI systems in support of sustainable development. It calls on Member States and other stakeholders to align AI development, deployment, and use with international human rights law, protect rights throughout the AI lifecycle, and strengthen international cooperation, especially for developing countries.
applies_to: All UN Member States and a broad set of stakeholders, including the private sector, civil society, research organizations, and the media; applies globally across AI systems and related governance activities, with special attention to developing countries and capacity-building.

full_name: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights: Making Automated Systems Work for the American People
issuing_body: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP)
scope_summary: This White House blueprint is a non-binding policy framework for automated systems that may meaningfully affect rights, opportunities, or access to critical resources or services. It is intended to guide governments at all levels, companies, and other organizations in designing, deploying, and overseeing AI and other automated systems in ways that protect civil rights, civil liberties, privacy, and democratic values.
applies_to: Any organization — public or private — that creates, deploys, oversees, or uses automated systems with the potential to meaningfully impact civil rights, civil liberties, privacy, equal opportunity, or access to critical resources or services. The framework is sector-agnostic and especially relevant to high-impact use cases in housing, employment, credit, education, healthcare, financial services, social services, safety, and government benefits.
